summary:
The Manchu Conquest of China (1618–1683 AD) was the process by which the Manchu people of northeastern China (Manchuria) overthrew the [[event:2374|Ming Dynasty]] and established the [[event:2337|Qing Dynasty]] — the last imperial dynasty of China — a conquest that took over six decades from the Manchu confederation's formation to the final subjugation of southern China and Taiwan. The Manchu were a Tungusic-speaking people who in the early 17th century transformed from a collection of semi-nomadic clans under Nurhaci (who founded the Later Jin state in 1616) into an organized military power using the banner system — a military-administrative structure dividing warriors and their families into eight banners, providing both battlefield organization and administrative governance.

The conquest was enabled by the [[event:2374|Ming Dynasty's]] internal collapse: the peasant rebel Li Zicheng captured Beijing in 1644, whereupon the Ming general Wu Sangui invited the Manchus through the Shanhaiguan pass to help defeat the rebels. The Manchus seized the opportunity, declared the Qing Dynasty, and rapidly moved against both the rebels and the remaining Ming loyalists in the south. The southern Ming courts resisted for nearly two more decades: the last mainland Ming pretender was defeated in 1662; the conquest of Taiwan (where [[event:3306|Koxinga's]] successors had established a Ming loyalist state) was completed in 1683. The Manchu rulers implemented deliberate policies to manage their rule over a Han Chinese majority far outnumbering them: the queue (shaved forehead, long braid) was imposed as a symbol of submission; Manchu and Chinese were kept in separate administrative tracks; and Han culture was simultaneously patronized and carefully monitored through the [[event:3511|literary inquisition]].
